summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's decision to ban roughly 5,000 'sexy apps' from the App Store while granting exceptions to established brands like Sports Illustrated, Playboy, and Victoria's Secret. He dismisses theories about revenue, iPad deals, education markets, and futile attempts to ban pornography from the iPhone entirely. Instead, he argues it's fundamentally about branding — Apple sees the App Store as an extension of its brand and was uncomfortable with the cheesy, skeevy vibe these apps were creating. However, he criticizes the hypocrisy of the brand-name exceptions and the capriciousness of the removals, arguing they undermine developer trust in the platform.
key_points: Apple's sexy app ban is about brand management, not revenue, education, or morality — the App Store is an extension of Apple's brand identity
The exceptions for Sports Illustrated, Playboy, and Victoria's Secret are hypocritical: apps should be evaluated by content, not by the reputation of who submits them
The sudden removal of previously-allowed apps unsettles all developers, not just those making bikini apps, because it reveals the App Store as a shaky business foundation
There is a fundamental conflict between Apple viewing the App Store as a branding vehicle and developers viewing it as the entirety of the Cocoa Touch platform
Safari's openness to any web content proves Apple isn't trying to ban objectionable content from the device — only from its storefront
money_quotes: Apple sees the App Store as an extension of the Apple brand. That's why flat-out pornography has never been and never will be allowed.

You can walk into a Barnes and Noble and buy a copy of Maxim, but you won't find a copy of Hustler. Not because Hustler wouldn't sell, but because selling pornography goes against the Barnes and Noble brand.
These sexy apps were casting the App Store into something junkier, bordering on the skeevy.
What iPhone users choose to access through MobileSafari doesn't reflect on Apple. But what is listed in the App Store does reflect on Apple.
If Apple's going to allow any of these apps, they ought to allow all of them. They should be evaluated by content, not by the names submitting them.
What developers see is that the App Store is a shaky foundation upon which to build a business. One day you're prospering, the next day your app is gone.
There are awesome iPhone OS apps that aren't being built because developers don't trust Apple not to yank the carpet out from underneath them.

It's entrepreneurism to be willing to take your chances in the market. It's healthy skepticism to worry about being locked out of the market after you've already invested heavily in building your product.
key_insight: Apple's App Store sexy-app purge reveals a fundamental tension: Apple treats the store as a brand extension it curates for image, while developers depend on it as their sole market access — and the capricious enforcement of brand standards erodes the trust that makes the platform worth building on.
